companyIntro.name: Prime Infrastructure
companyIntro.mission: Deliver durable road and bridge infrastructure that performs under real traffic loading and real climate — quality control is what makes a design's assumptions actually true on site.
companyIntro.culture: Checklist-driven and unforgiving of shortcuts. QA/QC engineers are expected to hold up a pour or a delivery if the paperwork doesn't match the material in front of them, no matter the schedule pressure.

projectBackground.name: Sumgait Industrial Access Upgrade — Pavement Package
projectBackground.location: Sumgait Industrial Access Road, Ch. 0+000 to 3+400
projectBackground.context: A delivery of sub-base aggregate has arrived on site with a supplier datasheet attached, and the site foreman wants to place it today to stay on programme. Before it goes down, QA/QC needs to confirm the material actually meets the specification, not just that paperwork exists.

manager.name: Nigar Hasanova
manager.note: The foreman wants this aggregate down by 2pm. That's not a reason to skip checking the datasheet against spec — if it doesn't meet grading requirements, it comes off site today, not after it's compacted under the sub-base above it.

assignment.title: Verify the sub-base aggregate delivery against specification
assignment.brief: Check the supplier's material datasheet against the pavement specification's grading and quality requirements, inspect a sample against the checklist, and issue an accept/reject decision before placement proceeds.

documents.name: Sub-Base Aggregate Specification (extract)
documents.note: Grading envelope: 100% passing 37.5mm sieve, 30-65% passing 4.75mm sieve, 5-15% passing 0.075mm sieve. Plasticity Index: max 6. CBR (soaked): min 30%.

documents.name: Supplier Material Datasheet — Delivery Batch #SB-114
documents.note: Grading: 100% passing 37.5mm, 58% passing 4.75mm, 19% passing 0.075mm. Plasticity Index: 5. CBR (soaked): 34%.

documents.name: Site Material Inspection Checklist — Template
documents.note: Items: datasheet present and current, grading within spec envelope, visual check for contamination/oversize material, sample retained for independent lab testing, delivery docket cross-referenced to datasheet batch number.

documents.name: Non-Conformance Report — Template
documents.note: Fields: item, specification requirement, actual result, disposition (accept/reject/accept with conditions), corrective action.

tasks.title: Grading Envelope Check
tasks.prompt: Comparing the supplier's datasheet (19% passing the 0.075mm sieve) against the specification (5-15% passing 0.075mm), what is the correct QA/QC decision?
options: Accept — 19% is close enough to the 15% upper limit
Reject — the fines content (19% passing 0.075mm) exceeds the specification's 15% maximum, which affects drainage and compaction behaviour
Accept, since the CBR and plasticity index both pass
No decision needed — grading only matters for the top pavement layer, not sub-base
tasks.reasoning: The specification sets a maximum of 15% passing the 0.075mm sieve (fines content) for sub-base material, and this batch tests at 19%, which is outside the allowed envelope. Excess fines reduce drainage capacity and can affect long-term compaction stability, regardless of the fact that CBR and plasticity index happen to pass — a material must satisfy every grading limit, not just the ones that pass.
tasks.tip: Always check every parameter against its own limit independently — a material can pass CBR and plasticity while still failing grading, and all relevant limits must be satisfied, not just a majority of them.
tasks.mistake: A common mistake is treating an overall 'looks mostly fine' impression as sufficient, when specifications are structured as a set of independent pass/fail thresholds where any single failure means the whole batch fails.
tasks.recommendation: When a single parameter fails just outside its limit, don't approve on the assumption it's a rounding or testing variance — request a retest or a second sample before making a final disposition, since a genuine 4-point overage on fines content is a real material difference, not noise.

tasks.title: Site Inspection Checklist
tasks.prompt: Before this batch can even be considered for placement, select every item that should be verified per the inspection checklist.
options: The datasheet is present, current, and matches this specific delivery batch number
The delivery truck's paint colour matches the supplier's usual livery
A visual check for contamination or oversize material in the load
A sample is retained for independent lab testing regardless of what the supplier's datasheet shows
The foreman's personal opinion on whether the material 'looks fine'
tasks.reasoning: A proper material inspection verifies the paperwork matches the actual delivered batch, checks for visible defects the datasheet wouldn't capture, and retains an independent sample — this last point matters because a QA/QC process shouldn't rely solely on the supplier's own self-reported figures. Truck livery and informal opinions about how material 'looks' are not verification steps.
tasks.tip: Always retain an independent sample for lab testing even when a supplier's datasheet looks compliant — datasheets describe a batch in general, but the specific truckload in front of you should still be independently verifiable.
tasks.mistake: Relying entirely on a supplier-provided datasheet without any independent sampling is a common shortcut that removes the actual verification value of the QA/QC process — it becomes paperwork-checking rather than quality assurance.
tasks.recommendation: Keep a retained-sample log cross-referenced to delivery docket numbers for every material batch — if a pavement performance issue surfaces later, this is the record that lets you trace it back to a specific delivery.

tasks.title: Non-Conformance Report
tasks.prompt: Write the NCR entry (3-5 sentences): the specification requirement, the actual result, and your disposition decision.
tasks.modelAnswer: Sub-base aggregate delivery batch #SB-114 was tested for grading per the pavement specification, which requires 5-15% passing the 0.075mm sieve; the supplier's datasheet reports 19% passing, exceeding the maximum by 4 percentage points, while CBR (34%) and plasticity index (5) both meet requirements. Given the fines content directly affects drainage performance and long-term compaction stability, this batch does not meet specification as delivered and cannot be placed. Disposition: reject this batch; request either a reworked/washed batch meeting the grading envelope or a formal design engineer waiver with supporting justification before any further material from this supplier lot is accepted.
tasks.reasoning: A complete NCR states the exact requirement, the actual measured result, and a clear disposition — this is what lets the supplier and site team both understand precisely what needs to change, rather than a vague 'material rejected' note that doesn't specify why or what would make it acceptable.
tasks.tip: Always state the specific numeric gap (here, 4 percentage points over the limit) rather than just 'fails grading' — a supplier can act on 'reduce fines by at least 4%' far more directly than on a general fail notice.
tasks.mistake: A weak NCR states that material 'doesn't meet spec' without identifying which specific parameter failed and by how much, which slows down the supplier's ability to correct and resupply.
tasks.recommendation: Keep every rejected batch's NCR on file cross-referenced to the delivery docket and supplier — a pattern of repeated grading failures from the same supplier is itself useful evidence for a broader procurement conversation.

tasks.title: Material Datasheet Currency Check
tasks.prompt: A second delivery arrives with a datasheet dated 14 months ago, for a supplier whose quality control certification requires datasheets to be refreshed every 12 months. What should QA/QC do?
options: Accept it — the material properties don't change over time
Treat the datasheet as expired and require a current one (or a fresh sample test) before accepting the delivery
Accept it only if the foreman is in a hurry
Reject the material permanently regardless of any future testing
tasks.reasoning: A datasheet's validity period exists because supplier quarry sources, processing methods, and batch consistency can change over time — an expired datasheet doesn't necessarily mean the material is non-compliant, but it does mean there's no currently valid evidence that it is, and the correct QA/QC response is to require fresh evidence, not to assume based on age alone in either direction.
tasks.tip: Treat a datasheet's expiry the same way you'd treat an expired calibration certificate on a test instrument — the underlying thing might still be fine, but you no longer have valid documented evidence of that, so the paperwork itself needs refreshing before you can rely on it.
tasks.mistake: Accepting a delivery based on an expired datasheet 'since nothing else has changed' undermines the entire purpose of a certification renewal period, which exists precisely to catch changes that aren't otherwise visible.
tasks.recommendation: Maintain a simple register of supplier datasheet expiry dates across all active material sources — catching an expiring datasheet before the next delivery arrives avoids a hold-up at the point of delivery.

tasks.title: Sampling Frequency Check
tasks.prompt: The QA/QC plan requires one independent sample per 500 tonnes of sub-base delivered. If 3,400 tonnes have been delivered so far this month, how many independent samples should have been taken (round up to the nearest whole sample)?
tasks.workingNote: 3,400 / 500 = 6.8, which rounds up to 7 samples, since a partial 500-tonne increment still requires its own sample.
tasks.reasoning: Sampling frequency requirements are based on rounding up any partial increment, since the purpose is to ensure no more than 500 tonnes goes unverified by an independent sample — rounding down would leave part of the delivered tonnage effectively unsampled.
tasks.tip: Always round sampling frequency calculations up, not to the nearest whole number — a QA/QC sampling plan exists to cap the maximum unverified quantity, and rounding down defeats that purpose even if it seems like a minor difference.
tasks.mistake: Rounding 6.8 down to 6 samples might seem like a reasonable approximation, but it leaves 400 tonnes of delivered material outside the required sampling coverage, which is exactly the gap the sampling frequency rule is designed to prevent.
tasks.recommendation: Track cumulative delivered tonnage against samples taken as a running tally, not just per-delivery — it's the only reliable way to catch a sampling shortfall before it accumulates across many small deliveries.

tasks.title: Batch Compliance Tracker (Excel)
tasks.prompt: Three more delivery batches need their grading results checked against the specification limits, with a clear pass/fail flag. Download the template, complete it in Excel, then upload it for automatic checking.

checks.label: Batch SB-115 (all three parameters within spec)
Batch SB-116 (70% passing 4.75mm exceeds the 65% maximum)
Batch SB-117 (16% fines exceeds 15% max, and PI of 7 exceeds max 6)

tasks.reasoning: A batch only passes if every single parameter is within its own specification limit — SB-116 fails despite fine grading and PI both being fine, purely because its 4.75mm passing percentage exceeds the maximum; SB-117 fails on two separate parameters independently. Building this as an explicit multi-condition check (not just eyeballing each column) is what a real batch compliance tracker needs to do reliably.
tasks.tip: Build the overall result as a formula checking every parameter against its own limit (e.g. an AND-style condition across all three columns) rather than manually eyeballing each row — it removes the risk of missing one failing parameter among several passing ones.
tasks.mistake: A common QA/QC tracking error is marking a batch as PASS after checking only one or two of the required parameters, missing a failure in a parameter that wasn't the focus of attention that day.
tasks.recommendation: Any batch tracker used for real project records should have its PASS/FAIL logic double-checked against a known example of each type (all-pass, single-parameter fail, multi-parameter fail) before being trusted for live decisions — exactly like the three rows in this exercise.

caseStudy.title: Case Study: The Datasheet That Was Right, Once
caseStudy.background: On an earlier Prime Infrastructure project, a QA/QC engineer accepted six consecutive deliveries from the same supplier based on a single datasheet from the first delivery, assuming later loads from the 'same source' would be identical — the fourth delivery had actually come from a different quarry face with materially higher fines content.
caseStudy.budget: Remediation cost: $145,000 for partial sub-base removal and replacement in the affected section
caseStudy.timeline: Discovered during a routine post-compaction proof-roll that showed unusual deflection in one section
caseStudy.challenges: Because the affected material had already been placed and compacted under subsequent layers, remediation required excavating back through completed work to reach and replace the non-compliant sub-base.
caseStudy.constraints: The supplier's contract didn't explicitly require a datasheet per individual delivery, only 'per source', which had been informally interpreted as covering the whole contract period rather than per batch — a gap that was only closed after this incident.
questions: Why can material from the 'same supplier' still vary significantly between deliveries?
What specific sampling practice would have caught the fourth delivery's difference?
How should a QA/QC engineer interpret an ambiguous contract requirement ('per source') when the stricter reading is safer?
caseStudy.solution: Prime Infrastructure amended its QA/QC procedure to require a datasheet and independent sample verification for every individual delivery batch, explicitly regardless of whether it comes from a supplier already approved earlier in the contract.
caseStudy.lessonsLearned: A supplier being 'approved' isn't the same as every individual batch being verified — natural material sources vary between extraction faces and over time, and a QA/QC process that stops checking after the first delivery from a known-good supplier is trusting an assumption rather than verifying a fact.

quiz.q: A sub-base material passes CBR and plasticity index but fails the grading envelope on fines content. The correct decision is:
options: Accept, since 2 of 3 parameters pass
Reject — every specification parameter must be satisfied independently
Accept with no further action needed
Ignore grading since CBR is the most important parameter

quiz.q: Why should an independent sample be retained even when a supplier's datasheet shows compliant results?
options: It's not necessary if the datasheet is compliant
QA/QC shouldn't rely solely on a supplier's self-reported figures for the specific batch delivered
Independent samples are only for non-compliant batches
Retained samples have no practical use

quiz.q: A sampling plan requires one sample per 500 tonnes; 3,400 tonnes have been delivered. How many samples are required?
In the case study, what was the core mistake that led to the $145,000 remediation?
options: The supplier was fraudulent
Later deliveries from the 'same supplier' were assumed identical to the first, without independent verification per batch
The specification was wrong
The proof-roll test was faulty

quiz.q: An expired supplier datasheet (past its required renewal period) should be treated as:
options: Still valid since properties don't change
No longer valid evidence — a current datasheet or fresh sample test is required before acceptance
Automatically a rejection of the material itself
Irrelevant to the QA/QC decision

prompts: Which task required you to reject a batch that passed most, but not all, of its requirements? Why is a single failing parameter enough to fail the whole batch?
The case study shows an assumption ('same supplier means same material') costing $145,000. Where else in engineering might a reasonable-sounding assumption substitute for actual verification?
If you were Nigar, what one rule would you add to the QA/QC procedure so this specific gap can never be exploited by schedule pressure again?

certificate.name: QA/QC Engineer — Material Verification & Batch Compliance
certificate.nameAz: Keyfiyyətə Nəzarət Mühəndisi — Material Doğrulaması və Partiya Uyğunluğu
